explanation: This specification states that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true (at exactly that time). In LTL, this is formulated as 'globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 is true.'

This statement means that prop_0 must happen whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true at the same time. In LTL, this is formalized by globally requiring that if prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_0 must also hold at that moment.
The statement indicates that 'prop_0' should happen (be true) at every point where both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are true. So, globally, whenever ('prop_1' & 'prop_2') holds, 'prop_0' must also hold.
Whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true at the same time. This is expressed globally: at all times, if prop_1 and prop_2 hold, so must prop_0.

The sentence is ambiguous but appears to mean that proposition prop_0 holds whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 hold. In LTL, this is captured by requiring that globally, if (prop_1 and prop_2) hold, then prop_0 must also hold at the same time.

The statement means that prop_0 becomes true whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true. This is usually captured by stating that globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_0 must also hold at the same time.

The statement indicates that 'prop_0' should occur when both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' hold. This can be interpreted as: Whenever both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are true, then 'prop_0' is also true at that time (globally).
This states that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_0 should also hold at that position. In other words, globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 is also true.

The sentence states that whenever prop_1 and prop_2 are true, the autopilot shall prop_0. This is a typical response property: globally (always), if prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_0 must also hold at that moment. In LTL this is expressed as G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).
Whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 hold, prop_0 must also hold at the same time. This is expressed by stating that globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must be true as well.
